content:
# Genesis Chapter 28 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 15v–16r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_028_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_028_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0028 (page 24), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0029 (page 25)

---

## The Stairway Between Worlds

**1** So Isaac called for Jacob, blessed him, and gave him these instructions: "Don't take a wife from the Canaanite women. **2** Go now to Paddan-aram, to the household of Bethuel — your mother's father. Take a wife there from among the daughters of your uncle Laban. **3** May El Shaddai bless you, make you fruitful, and multiply your descendants until they become a great assembly of peoples. **4** May he give you the blessing of Abraham — to you and to your children after you — so that you may take possession of the land where you have lived as a foreigner, the land God gave to Abraham."

**5** So Isaac sent Jacob on his way, and Jacob went to Paddan-aram, to Laban the son of Bethuel the Aramean — the brother of Rebekah, who was Jacob's and Esau's mother.

---

**6** When Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him off to Paddan-aram to find a wife there, and that when he blessed him he had commanded Jacob, "Don't take a wife from among the Canaanite women" — **7** and that Jacob had obeyed his father and his mother and gone to Paddan-aram — **8** Esau realized that the Canaanite women were displeasing to his father Isaac. **9** So Esau went to Ishmael and took as his wife Mahalath, the daughter of Abraham's son Ishmael, and sister of Nebaioth — in addition to the wives he already had.

---

**10** Jacob set out from Beersheba and headed toward Haran. **11** He came to a certain place and stopped there for the night, because the sun had gone down. He took one of the stones lying there, put it under his head, and lay down to sleep.

**12** And he dreamed.

There before him stood a stairway with its foot on the ground and its top reaching up into heaven — and on it, the angels of God were going up and coming down. **13** And right there, at the top, the LORD was standing. He said:

"I am the LORD, the God of your father Abraham and the God of Isaac. The land where you are lying — I will give it to you and to your descendants. **14** Your descendants will be as countless as the dust of the earth: they will spread out to the west and to the east, to the north and to the south. And through you and your offspring, every family on earth will be blessed.

**15** "Know this: I am with you. I will watch over you wherever you go, and I will bring you back to this land. I will not leave you until I have done everything I have promised you."

---

**16** Jacob woke from his sleep and said, "Surely the LORD is in this place — and I had no idea."

**17** He was afraid. "How awe-inspiring is this place!" he said. "This is nothing less than the house of God. This is the very gate of heaven."

---

**18** Early the next morning Jacob took the stone he had used as his pillow, set it upright as a standing pillar, and poured oil on top of it. **19** He named that place Bethel — though the town had previously been called Luz.

**20** Then Jacob made a vow: "If God will be with me and watch over me on this journey I am taking — if he will give me food to eat and clothes to wear — **21** and if I return safely to my father's house — then the LORD will be my God. **22** And this stone that I have set up as a pillar will be God's house. And of everything you give me, I will give back to you a full tenth."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The shape of the chapter

Genesis 28 falls into three movements: the departure (vv.1-9), the dream (vv.10-19), and the vow (vv.20-22). The first movement is almost businesslike — Isaac sends Jacob north with two things: a blessing and instructions about marriage. Sandwiched inside is the little tragedy of Esau's belated attempt to please his parents (vv.6-9). Then Jacob leaves Beersheba, and with the setting of the sun, everything changes. The middle movement — the dream theophany — is the centerpiece of the chapter and one of the most visually arresting moments in all of Genesis. The third movement is Jacob's response: the naming of Bethel and the conditional vow that reveals as much about Jacob's psychology as any verse in the book.

---

### Verse 3 — El Shaddai

**וְאֵל שַׁדַּי יְבָרֵךְ אֹתְךָ** — "And El Shaddai bless you." The divine name **אֵל שַׁדַּי** (*El Shaddai*) is the name by which the ancestral God is characteristically known in the patriarchal narratives (compare Genesis 17:1, 35:11, 43:14, 49:25; Exodus 6:3). Its meaning is debated — the traditional rendering "God Almighty" goes back to the LXX (**ὁ θεός σου** in some manuscripts, though Vaticanus renders it here as **ὁ θεός μου**, "my God") and the Vulgate's *Omnipotens*. Other proposals include "God of the mountains," "God who is sufficient," or a divine epithet from Akkadian *shaddu* (mountain). None is certain. What is clear in context is that this is the name of the God who blesses with fertility and multitude — his speech here echoes the Abrahamic covenant of Genesis 17. I have preserved El Shaddai untranslated in the text, as the name carries its own weight and has passed into the English religious vocabulary. "God Almighty" flattens something that the text leaves deliberately archaic and powerful.

The Leningrad Codex reads the name with full Masoretic vocalization: **אֵל שַׁדַּי** — the divine epithet in the absolute state. The Vaticanus LXX on page 24 renders the passage in running uncial script; the equivalent verses are legible in the center column.

---

### Verses 6-9 — Esau's sad coda

These four verses are often passed over quickly, but they reveal something important. Esau watches Jacob leave with his father's blessing and his father's instructions, and for the first time Esau does something designed not for himself but to please his parents. He recognizes — too late — that the Canaanite women have grieved his father. So he goes to Ishmael's house and takes another wife. The detail that it is *in addition* to his existing wives only deepens the pathos. He is trying to correct the record, but the record cannot be corrected. The son who lived for himself, who sold his birthright for a bowl of soup and married outside the covenant community without a second thought, is now scrambling to show that he too can follow the family's values. It does not register. The narrative moves on without comment. Esau cannot catch up; he can only fall further behind.

---

### Verse 11 — "a certain place" (*makom*)

**וַיִּפְגַּע בַּמָּקוֹם** — literally, "he encountered the place." The Hebrew verb **פָּגַע** (*paga*) normally means to come upon or encounter — sometimes in the sense of meeting an obstacle, sometimes in the sense of intercession. Here it introduces the site of the theophany with unusual weight: this is not merely a campsite Jacob chose; it is a place he *encountered*, almost as though the place found him. Later Jewish interpretation read **הַמָּקוֹם** (*ha-makom*, "the Place") as a divine epithet — one of the rabbinic names for God, based on the idea that God is the place of the world and the world is not his place. Whether that reading is in view here is debated, but the choice of *paga* makes this feel like something more than a random halt.

The sun going down frames the transition: Jacob is moving from the ordinary world into the threshold between worlds, and night is when that threshold opens.

---

### Verse 12 — *sullam* (סֻלָּם): stairway, not ladder

**וְהִנֵּה סֻלָּם מֻצָּב אַרְצָה** — "And look: a *sullam*, set up on the ground." The word **סֻלָּם** (*sullam*) appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. Its traditional translation as "ladder" goes back to the LXX's **κλίμαξ** (*klimax*, a ladder or staircase) and the Vulgate's *scala*. But most modern scholars believe the more accurate image is a broad stairway or ramp — possibly evoking the great ziggurats of Mesopotamia, with their external staircases connecting earth to a summit-temple. The word appears to derive from the root **סָלַל** (*salal*) — to heap up, to build a ramp or causeway. Given that Jacob is in transit from Canaan toward Mesopotamia, the Babylonian architectural imagination is a plausible backdrop for his dream. He falls asleep in the open country and dreams of a cosmic ziggurat — a structure designed precisely to connect the human world to the divine.

The image I have used — "stairway" — follows modern scholarship (compare NIV, NRSV) and better captures the grandeur of the vision: not a flimsy household ladder but a broad ascending way, solid and architectural, connecting earth and sky.

**וְרֹאשׁוֹ מַגִּיעַ הַשָּׁמָיְמָה** — "and its top reached the heavens." The structure is vast. Its foot rests on earth; its head (*rosh*) touches the sky. The Leningrad Codex script is clear on both ends of this image in verse 12.

---

### Verse 12 — ascending first

**וְהִנֵּה מַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים עֹלִים וְיֹרְדִים בּוֹ** — "and the angels of God were going up and coming down on it." The order is significant: *ascending first, then descending*. The angels are going *up* before they come *down*. This means they were already on earth when Jacob saw them — they were not descending from heaven into the world, but moving in both directions as active mediators. They were already with him, already present in the world, and the stairway was the visible axis along which that invisible traffic moved. The dream makes the hidden reality visible: the world Jacob thought was ordinary is already populated by divine messengers.

The Vaticanus LXX reflects the same word order: **ἀναβαίνοντες καὶ καταβαίνοντες** — ascending and descending — following the Hebrew faithfully.

---

### Verse 13 — "above it / beside him" (*'alav*)

**וְהִנֵּה יְהוָה נִצָּב עָלָיו** — "And look: the LORD was standing *'alav*." The pronoun **עָלָיו** (*'alav*) is grammatically ambiguous: it can mean "above it" (above the stairway) or "beside him" (beside Jacob). The stairway (*sullam*) is grammatically masculine, and so is Jacob; the pronoun could refer to either. Older translations favor "above it" — the LORD at the top of the cosmic stairway, presiding over the vision from heaven. More recent interpreters have argued for "beside him" — the LORD standing next to the sleeping Jacob on the ground, personally present in an intimate act of accompaniment.

The theological weight differs significantly. "Above it" makes this a vision of divine sovereignty over cosmic order; "beside him" makes it a scene of personal accompaniment to a frightened runaway. I have chosen "at the top" — not because the ambiguity is resolved, but because the spatial logic of the whole vision (a structure whose head is in heaven) suggests the LORD appears at the structure's summit, even as the promise he speaks is emphatically personal and close. The note of intimacy comes in verse 15 regardless: "I am with you."

---

### Verse 14 — the universal blessing again

**וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כָּל-מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה** — "and through you all the families of the earth will bless themselves/be blessed." The verb **נִבְרְכוּ** is Niphal — passive or reflexive: "be blessed" or "bless themselves." This formula appears in the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 12:3, 18:18, 22:18) and is here transmitted to Jacob. The passive reading gives a promise: every human family will receive blessing through Jacob's line. The reflexive reading gives a different image: people will use Jacob's name as a standard of blessing, saying "May you be like Jacob." Both readings honor the Hebrew; the passive has generally prevailed in Christian reception (see the NT's use in Galatians 3:8).

---

### Verse 15 — the promise to a fugitive

**וְהִנֵּה אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ** — "Look — I myself am with you." The personal pronoun **אָנֹכִי** (*anoki*) adds emphasis that the more common *ani* does not carry; it is used at heightened emotional or covenantal moments. The LORD is not merely speaking to Jacob; he is making a personal commitment to this particular man at this particular vulnerable moment. Jacob is a runaway — a deceiver fleeing a brother who wants to kill him, heading into the unknown with nothing but a stone for a pillow. The divine promise here does not ignore that context. It speaks into it. "Wherever you go" — the LORD is not confining his presence to the land of promise. He will be with Jacob in Haran, in exile, in the strange house of Laban. The promise is not geographical; it is relational.

**עַד אֲשֶׁר אִם-עָשִׂיתִי אֵת אֲשֶׁר-דִּבַּרְתִּי לָךְ** — "until I have done everything I have promised you." This is a covenant oath: the LORD commits not to withdraw until completion. It is, in the context of Jacob's whole life, a promise that spans twenty years of foreign labor and family strife. The reader who knows the Jacob story understands that the fulfillment of this promise will be long, strange, and sometimes barely recognizable as divine care. But it will be there.

---

### Verse 16 — the great recognition

**אָכֵן יֵשׁ יְהוָה בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה וְאָנֹכִי לֹא יָדָעְתִּי** — "Surely the LORD is in this place — and I did not know it."

This is one of the most honest sentences in the book of Genesis. It is the opposite of religious pretension. Jacob wakes up to reality: the LORD was already present in the very place where Jacob happened to be, and Jacob had gone to sleep completely unaware of it. The word **אָכֵן** (*aken*) — "surely, truly, indeed" — marks the statement as a sudden recognition, a moment of arrival at certainty. The *lo yadati* — "I did not know" — is not a confession of sin but an admission of ignorance. The world was already charged with presence; Jacob simply did not know.

The sentence forms a kind of compressed theology of encounter: God does not wait to be sought before being present. He was there before Jacob. He will be there after Jacob leaves. The dream was not Jacob finding God but God interrupting Jacob's sleep to show him what was already true.

---

### Verse 17 — Bethel: the house of God and the gate of heaven

**מַה-נּוֹרָא הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה** — "How awe-inspiring is this place!" The root **יָרֵא** (*yare*) covers the full range from *fear* to *reverence* to *awe*. Jacob is not merely impressed; he is seized with the shudder of sacred encounter. What he feels is the numinous — the *mysterium tremendum* that Rudolf Otto described as the core religious experience: the overwhelming sense of a power that is wholly other, that commands and terrifies and draws all at once.

**בֵּית אֱלֹהִים** (*bet Elohim*) — "the house of God" — and **שַׁעַר הַשָּׁמַיִם** (*sha'ar ha-shamayim*) — "the gate of heaven." Two titles for the same place. The stairway Jacob saw connects the two: below is the house (the earthly entry point, the place of encounter) and above is the gate (the threshold of the divine realm itself). Bethel — which Jacob will name in v.19 — means simply "house of God," *bet-El*. In naming the place, Jacob is not inventing but recognizing: this is what this place has always been.

The Vaticanus LXX on page 25 renders these phrases clearly as **οἶκος θεοῦ** and **πύλη τοῦ οὐρανοῦ**, preserving the architectural metaphors faithfully.

---

### Verse 18 — the standing stone (*massebah*)

**וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתָהּ מַצֵּבָה** — "he set it up as a standing pillar." The **מַצֵּבָה** (*massebah*) is a standing stone — a common form of memorial and sacred marker in the ancient Near East, erected at places of divine encounter or to mark covenants. Jacob anoints it with oil, marking it as sacred property, a monument to the encounter. Later biblical law would prohibit masseboth as idolatrous (Leviticus 26:1, Deuteronomy 16:22), but in the patriarchal period they function as legitimate markers of divine presence — the physical trace of an encounter that has no other form to leave behind.

The act of anointing with oil (*vayyitzok shemen me-rosho* — "he poured oil from its head") is the earliest anointing in the Bible: it consecrates not a king or a priest but a stone, as evidence of the place's sanctity. The English word "Messiah" (*mashiach*, "anointed one") derives from the same root. Jacob's first act of consecration in the Bible is pouring oil on a rock in the wilderness.

---

### Verses 20-22 — Jacob's conditional vow

**וַיִּדַּר יַעֲקֹב נֶדֶר** — "And Jacob made a vow." The vow that follows has generated centuries of theological controversy, because it is structured as a conditional: *if* God does these things for me, *then* I will acknowledge him as my God and give him a tenth of everything. Readers since the early church have asked: is this faith, or is it bargaining? Is Jacob worshipping or negotiating?

The answer is probably both, and the text makes no effort to hide the ambiguity. This is Jacob — the man who grabbed his brother's heel in the womb, who traded soup for a birthright, who deceived his blind father to steal a blessing. He has just had the most dramatic divine encounter of anyone in Genesis to this point, and his response is to offer God a deal. He will pledge loyalty in exchange for provision and safe return. The conditionality is real: the *if* is not merely rhetorical.

And yet. The vow is not cynical. Jacob has just heard the LORD promise to be with him, to keep him, to bring him back. The vow is Jacob's way of responding to that promise — not with smooth certainty but with honest contingency. He will see how this goes. If it is as God says — *then* he will be faithful. It is the beginning of a relationship, not the culmination of one.

**וְהָאֶבֶן הַזֹּאת אֲשֶׁר-שַׂמְתִּי מַצֵּבָה יִהְיֶה בֵּית אֱלֹהִים** — "And this stone which I have set up as a pillar will be the house of God." Jacob echoes his own naming of the place in v.19: what he named Bethel in recognition, he now consecrates in vow. The stone is the placeholder for a house. Later Jacob will return and do more (Genesis 35:14-15). For now, a stone and a vow are what he has.

**וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר תִּתֶּן-לִי עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ לָךְ** — "and of everything you give me, I will give back to you a full tenth." The tithe — here appearing for the first time in the Jacob narrative, and one of the earliest instances in the Bible (compare Abraham's tithe to Melchizedek in Genesis 14:20) — is Jacob's acknowledgment that everything he has will have been gift. The return of a tenth is not payment; it is the formal recognition that the giver remains the owner.

---

### The theology of this chapter: encounter at the border

Genesis 28 is a threshold chapter in every sense. Jacob crosses a geographic threshold (Beersheba to Haran), a temporal threshold (leaving his family's world forever), and a spiritual threshold (from the God of his fathers to his own direct encounter with the LORD). The dream happens at the border — not at a temple, not in a city, not in the presence of a priest, but in the open country at sunset, where a runaway sleeps on a stone.

The great claim of the chapter — the claim that will drive the entire theology of the Jacob cycle — is this: the God of Abraham and Isaac is not anchored to a particular holy site. He can appear in an empty field to a man lying on the ground. And when Jacob wakes, the ordinary world is irrevocably different: the place he thought was just a place turns out to be the house of God. The gate of heaven is not somewhere else. It is here.

The Leningrad Codex page 1 (folio 15v) carries the text from approximately verse 1 through verse 15 in the right and center columns, with the continuation of Genesis 28 running into the final column of that folio. The chapter concludes on page 2 (folio 16r), with verse 16 through verse 22 visible in the opening columns before Genesis 29 begins. The Masoretic cantillation marks — the team and etnahta accents in particular — rhythmically segment the divine speech of verses 13-15 in a way that mirrors its covenantal weight. The chapter heading is clearly marked in the Leningrad Codex with the standard parasha break notation before verse 10, signaling the beginning of the Bethel narrative as a distinct literary unit.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 15v–16r, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 28 occupies the final columns of folio 15v (verses 1–15) and the opening columns of folio 16r (verses 16–22) before Genesis 29 begins. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 24–25 (accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0028 and /0029). The Vaticanus LXX follows the Hebrew closely throughout Genesis 28. Notable observations from the Greek: v.12 — Vaticanus reads **κλίμαξ** (klimax, a staircase/ladder), the standard LXX rendering of sullam; v.13 — the LORD's position reads **ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς** in the LXX ("on/above it"), supporting the reading "above the stairway" rather than "beside him"; v.14 — the LXX uses **ἐνευλογηθήσονται** (they will be blessed), the passive form, committing to the passive rather than the reflexive reading of the Niphal; v.17 — the awe-saying is rendered as **ὡς φοβερὸς ὁ τόπος οὗτος** ("how fearful is this place"), using phoberos (frightening/awesome); v.19 — the LXX names the former city Ουλαμμαους (Oulammaous) for Luz, a variant spelling. No DSS fragments of Genesis 28 are extant.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
